headline: Tom is quietly pulling back, and the design-system PR became the place where that retreat turned into dread.
summary_bullets: Tom's disengagement is broader than one bad PR: he says he is "usually scrolling at 11pm trying to not think about work," which ties the work issue to a real off-hours avoidance pattern.
The clarity problem is not abstract; he does the work on his board but "can't really tell what's important vs busywork," so the current priority system is not landing for him.
The silence around stretch is telling: he skipped the growth question entirely, which fits a junior engineer who is no longer leaning forward and may be protecting himself from more exposure.
understanding_paragraph: The session confirmed that Tom is not just behind; he is carrying anxiety into the work and into the evening. What was new is how much the design-system PR has become a psychological blocker — he has been "putting off the follow-up" and feels "a bit sick" when he opens it. Tom also revealed that he is not asking for feedback because he does not want to seem incompetent, which means he is hiding uncertainty instead of using the support around him.

axes.meaning: Weak signal, not actionable on its own: Tom is "kind of drained" and not sleeping well, but the session did not separate fatigue from stress or a broader life issue.
This is a real pattern, act on it: Tom is avoiding the work, staying up late to not think about it, and the energy that used to be obvious is gone.
Worth noting, watch over the next few weeks: Tom knows the tasks in front of him, but he does not know what matters, and the review process is still not legible to him.
Weak signal, not actionable on its own: he gave almost no signal about stretch or learning, which reads more like withdrawal than a clear plateau.

brutal_truth_employee: Tom is not just confused; he is avoiding the work because it now makes him feel "a bit sick." The line "I don't want to seem like I don't know what I'm doing" says he is hiding uncertainty instead of getting the feedback that would help him recover.
brutal_truth_manager: You did the right thing by naming the design-system PR, but the conversation needed one sharper turn after "Every time I open it I feel a bit sick." That was the moment to stop exploring and ask what exactly in the feedback or the review culture made him feel unsafe, because that is the real issue under the slowdown.

next_actions.action: Email Tom a short note that the design-system PR is not a test of competence and ask him to reply with the two review comments that felt hardest to absorb.
Schedule a 30-minute reset with Tom to rewrite the next step on the design-system PR into a smaller, named deliverable and confirm who will review it.
Set up a recurring 15-minute priority check-in with Tom and force-rank his top three tasks so he stops living on whatever is on his board.
Lead with the question "what are you avoiding right now?" and stay on the design-system PR until Tom names the specific feedback that made him freeze.
Pair Tom with one senior frontend reviewer for a low-stakes feedback loop so he practices asking for critique without treating it like a competence test.

watch_for: If Tom keeps saying he is "behind on things" but still does not reopen the design-system PR, the avoidance is now the story.
If he does not ask for feedback in the next two weeks after you explicitly normalize it, his fear of looking incompetent is running the show.
If the next social or informal team moment is also skipped, the disconnection is broader than workload and needs a direct check-in.
